channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Engineering] TBM 417: Before You Fire All Your Glue People Because of AI
item.description:
John Cutler examines how organizations misapply AI heuristics to invisible coordination work and end up stripping out the people who preserve context, follow-through, and cross-team coherence. It is worth keeping because it gives a durable test for deciding which work AI can accelerate and which work still depends on human glue.

  • Visible artifacts are a weak proxy for the coordination work underneath them; replacing the person while keeping the artifact can hollow out the system.
  • AI is strongest on work that teams already agree should happen and can describe clearly; ambiguous cross-functional maintenance needs a higher skepticism level.
  • Staff reductions driven by surface-level heuristics can create delayed structural failures that only appear after the coordinating people are gone.
  • Evaluate candidate AI substitutions by tracing who currently creates context, drives follow-through, and resolves edge-case ambiguity.

item.title: [Informatics] A Well-Designed JavaScript Module System is Your First Architecture Decision
item.description:
Victor Amejimaobari argues that JavaScript modules define system boundaries, dependency direction, and team interfaces, then uses module graphs, barrel files, and coupling failures to show how module choices become architectural decisions. It is worth keeping because it turns everyday import and export choices into a concrete frontend maintainability model.

  • ESM static imports improve analyzability and tree-shaking, so module-system constraints shape build behavior as much as runtime behavior.
  • Module graphs expose circular dependencies, overloaded utility modules, and boundary violations early enough to prevent large refactors later.
  • Barrel files trade import convenience for build-time and bundle-size costs that become material in large frontends.
  • Module boundaries tend to mirror team boundaries, so ownership and communication patterns show up directly in architecture.

item.title: [Programming] Capacity Efficiency at Meta: How Unified AI Agents Optimize Performance at Hyperscale
item.description:
Meta describes a unified agent platform for performance engineering that combines standardized tools with domain-specific skills, then reuses the same foundation for regression mitigation and proactive optimization. It is worth keeping because it is one of the clearest large-scale examples of AI agents as infrastructure instead of chat wrappers.

  • A shared tool layer plus separate skill layers lets one agent platform support both reactive incident work and proactive optimization work.
  • Encoded expert reasoning shortens the path from regression signal to ready-to-review fix, which removes investigation as the main scaling bottleneck.
  • The platform design centers on reusable context gathering, validation criteria, and guardrails, which makes new agent workflows cheaper to add.
  • Reviewing AI-generated fixes can free senior engineers from repetitive triage and move their time toward higher-leverage product work.

item.title: [Programming] Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration at Meta: Framework, Lessons, and Takeaways
item.description:
Meta lays out a post-quantum migration framework built around prioritization, cryptographic inventory, external dependency tracking, guardrails, and staged maturity levels from awareness to deployment. It is worth keeping because it turns a vague future-security concern into an operational migration program.

  • PQC readiness starts with inventory and prioritization, because teams cannot migrate what they have not mapped or ranked.
  • Migration levels provide a planning language for partial progress, which helps teams move before every dependency is resolved.
  • Guardrails matter alongside rollout: deprecating vulnerable key generation and API usage reduces fresh quantum debt during the migration.
  • Hybrid deployments provide a practical safety margin while PQC standards, hardware support, and implementations continue to mature.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Reality check around requirements management
item.description:
This r/ExperiencedDevs thread describes a long-lived enterprise system whose requirements were reverse-engineered into a structured repository, then allowed to decay back into scattered files, oral history, and dead tooling. It is worth keeping because it captures the operational cost of treating system behavior knowledge as optional documentation instead of core infrastructure.

  • Requirements knowledge decays quickly when the steady-state editing workflow is weaker than the original transformation workflow.
  • Deleting old trackers and document stores before migration completes destroys the recovery paths teams rely on for edge-case behavior.
  • Large domain-heavy systems accumulate month-long investigation cycles when behavior, rationale, and test intent are scattered across people and files.
  • Living specifications are a delivery asset for support, onboarding, testing, and safe change management.
